title: The Paper Gown and the Psoas: Navigating Healthcare System Rage
dek: When the doctor dismisses your pain as anxiety, your jaw remembers. This is not a failure of patience; it is the biology of being unheard in a sterile room.
bodyMarkdown:
## Healthcare System Rage as a Patient: Introduction


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:42.965Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You sit in the plastic chair that scratches at your calves, the paper beneath you crinkling with every slight shift. It’s midafternoon on a Tuesday, the fluorescent lights humming with a brittle, artificial buzz overhead. The scent is antiseptic, a mixture of disinfectant and stale air recycled endlessly through vents that hum in tune with your rising tension. You press your palms into your lap, feeling the subtle tremor in your fingers  -  a silent warning from muscles tightening beneath your skin. Your trapezius, that stubborn web of muscle from neck to shoulder, knots into a brace. It’s the body’s way of saying, “Prepare.” Prepare for the dismissal, the invisible shrug behind the doctor’s eyes, the impatience that curls around your ribs like a noose.

This is not just impatience or momentary frustration. What you’re feeling is a biological response, a deep and ancient message from your body. Healthcare system rage is a language of muscle, fascia, and nerve endings, not a failure of your character or your ability to stay calm. Your masseter clenches, jaw taut as if to hold back a scream or a plea. The diaphragm, that dome of muscle beneath your lungs, flattens, restricting breath, grounding the fury in your chest. This is the body saying no when your mind cannot.

Gabor Maté, the physician and author whose work courses through the undercurrents of trauma and chronic illness, teaches us that when the mind is overwhelmed, when the system fails to hear us, the body takes up the burden of communication. “The body says no,” he writes, not as a metaphor but as a literal fact of biology. The ache in your lower back, the tightness in your neck, the flutter in your gut  -  these are not random. They are the somatic echoes of neglect, dismissal, and the suffocating weight of being unseen in a system built to streamline, not to listen.

Your body remembers every slight - every time your symptoms were minimized, every glance at a watch, every hurried note scribbled without eye contact. The levator scapulae muscles along your neck stiffen, the fascia around them thickening with each encounter where your pain was offhandedly dismissed or your concerns labeled as “psychosomatic.” This is not pathology; this is biology. Your rage is a signal, a flare shot from the depths of your nervous system, alerting you and the world that something fundamental has been denied.

You breathe shallowly, the cool clinical air filling your nostrils and mixing with the faint, almost imperceptible musk of your own stress sweat. The body does not lie or exaggerate. It stores, it remembers, and it reacts. In this sterile room, with its cold light and indifferent chairs, your rage is an embodied truth. It is a signal that the system’s failure to hold space for your whole self - the living, breathing, feeling you - is not just an oversight but a wound.

Here, in this moment, your rage is not a moral failing. It is a vital, necessary response from the tissues and cells within you. It is a call for recognition, a demand that the body’s voice be heard when the mind’s pleas have gone unanswered. This is healthcare system rage: fierce, biological, and utterly justified.

## The Waiting Room Body

You settle into the waiting room chair, a rigid plastic seat with a thin, vinyl cushion that presses cold and unyielding against your flesh. The antiseptic tang - sharp and sterile - hits your nose first, a biting reminder that this is not a space of comfort but a holding pen for your body’s unrest. The air conditioning hums overhead, a relentless, cool draft that seeps through your clothes and stiffens the skin along your arms, tightening the fascia over your triceps. The chill is a physical punctuation mark, a subtle but persistent irritation that you can’t ignore.

Your body braces without permission. The psoas, that deep core muscle nestled behind the abdomen and wrapping front-to-back around the lumbar spine, clenches as if preparing you to spring away from this moment of suspended time. It’s a slow, low burn, a tightening that begins almost imperceptibly but grows with every tick of the clock you hear faintly through the sterile walls. The minutes stretch, elongate, as you sit still, but your psoas won’t let you. It holds you captive in this small, shrinking space, a silent scream in muscle fibers that want to move, to escape, to be anywhere else but here. This is biology, not failure, a tightening wired into your survival.

Your breathing hitches into the upper chest, shallow and jagged, the diaphragm withdrawing its usual deep, rolling rhythm. Instead of the belly rising and falling with calm assurance, you find yourself inhaling short, sharp breaths, the levator scapulae and trapezius muscles in your neck hitching upward with each inhale. Your shoulders creep toward your ears, as if trying to protect you from the cold or the uncertainty that lingers in the stale air. The upper chest breath feels tight and constricted, like a bird trapped beneath a glass dome - frantic but futile. The body is alert, on edge, though the mind tries to summon patience.

Your jaw clamps shut; the masseter muscle clenches in a lock so firm it’s as though your teeth are grinding an invisible substance into dust. You might not even realize you’re doing it until your cheek muscles ache or a dull headache blooms behind your eyes. This is the body’s way of holding in what cannot be voiced - the indignation, the quiet fury of waiting while the clock mocks you. Every minute that ticks by without acknowledgment from the receptionist, without the arrival of the practitioner, tightens that jaw further, hardening the contours of your face into a mask of contained rage.

The waiting room smells like antiseptic and recycled air, but underneath that clinical overlay is the faint musk of human impatience - sweat, a trace of perfume, the subtle sourness of frustration. The fluorescent lights overhead flicker occasionally, casting a sterile glow that makes your skin feel paler, more exposed. You are here, but you are also nowhere, suspended in this liminal space where time drags and your body remembers every moment spent unseen, unheard, uncared for.

This is not pathology. This is biology. The body knows the insult of forgotten time, the betrayal of care deferred. It tightens the psoas, hitches the breath, locks the jaw, not because it wants to punish you, but because it is speaking - loud and clear - about what it needs, about what has been denied. And you, in this waiting room body, bear the weight of that anger as surely as you bear your own skin.

## The Dismissal and the Jaw

You’ve been sitting in that hard plastic chair for what feels like hours. The fluorescent lights hum with a sterile insistence, humming low and constant like a mantra of indifference. Then the door opens and the doctor walks in, eyes already downcast toward your chart, fingers flicking the pages as if your story is nothing more than ink on paper. When you finally speak, the words you’ve carried all day - aching, raw, desperate - are met with a swift, clinical dismissal: “It’s probably just stress,” or “Sounds like anxiety.” The verdict lands like a slap, but it’s not just your dignity that stings. Your body reacts first, fast and unbidden.

That heat rises immediately in your neck, a flush creeping beneath the skin like a slow-burning fire. It pools in the levator scapulae, that little muscle nestled high along the side of your neck, the one that drags your shoulder blade upward when you shrug off the world. You feel it coil tighter, knotting the fascia where muscle and connective tissue meet, a silent scream in the fibers that hold your head to your spine. Your trapezius joins the chorus, stiffening into a rigid wall of tension, pressing the weight of your frustration into your shoulders. It’s not just metaphor; your body is speaking in its own untranslatable language, a language that Bessel van der Kolk has spent decades helping us understand.

In *The Body Keeps the Score*, van der Kolk teaches us that these moments - the micro-traumas - do not disappear once the door closes behind the doctor. They lodge in your tissues, in the memory of your fascia, in the tightening of your jaw’s masseter muscle as you grit your teeth to keep the tears at bay. Your body remembers the invalidation long after your mind tries to move on. This is not pathology. This is biology. The body knows what the mind struggles to accept. That tightening in your jaw isn’t stubbornness; it’s the body’s way of protecting itself from yet another wound. The masseter clenches as if to bite down on the humiliation, the rawness of being unseen and unheard.

At 3 a.m., when the world is quiet and your thoughts echo loudest, you might notice the ache deep in your neck, the way your levator scapulae holds a silent grudge, refusing to release the tension because it carries more than just muscle strain - it carries your story. The dismissal from the doctor is not an isolated event; it is a thread woven into the tapestry of your lived experience, a repeated micro-trauma that your body catalogs with unerring precision. This is why the anger rises in your throat, why your breath catches in your diaphragm, why you feel simultaneously exhausted and wired - your nervous system trapped in the crossfire of disbelief and rage.

You are not imagining the weight of this dismissal. Your body is recording it with every tightening, every flare of heat, every clench of the jaw. This is the somatic truth of healthcare system rage - a fury born not just from external injustice but from the internal, physical reckoning that follows when your pain is denied, minimized, or pathologized. And just as the levator scapulae remembers, so too must you learn to listen.

## The Biology of Being Unheard

You sit on a stiff exam table, the cold vinyl pressing into your thighs, the antiseptic tang clinging to your nasal passages like a stubborn fog. You tell your story again - the ache in your jaw, the grinding in your masseter muscle that wakes you before dawn, the way your chest constricts like a vise around the diaphragm when you try to catch your breath. But the doctor’s eyes glaze over. Your symptoms are minimized, dismissed as anxiety or “all in your head.” This - this moment of being unseen, unheard - is not just a slight against your dignity. It’s a full-body event, a biological crisis written in the ancient language of your nervous system.

When your experience is invalidated, your sympathetic nervous system - the ancient fight-flight-freeze apparatus wired into your brainstem and spinal cord - kicks into overdrive. This isn’t pathology. It’s biology. Your body remembers the primal truth: when your pain, your reality, your voice is ignored, you are at risk. The levator scapulae along your neck tightens, pulling your shoulders up near your ears, your trapezius muscles knotting as if bracing for a blow. Your heart hammers a frantic rhythm, sending adrenaline flooding through your veins, sharpening your senses, preparing you either to fight against the invisibility or flee from the space where you are not seen. This cascade of stress chemicals is what fuels the fierce protective roar that rises in your throat, the anger that surges through your limbs like wildfire.

Anger here is not a moral failing or an embarrassing outburst. It is a messenger, a body-first signal trying to keep you safe in a system that repeatedly overlooks your truth. As Karla McLaren teaches, emotions are “information in motion.” Your rage pulses through your nervous system to say: “Pay attention. I am here. I matter.” When your pain is cast aside as psychosomatic, your brain’s amygdala - the sentinel of threat detection - alters your entire physiology. Your psoas muscles, those deep core stabilizers that cradle your lumbar spine, tense and shorten, anchoring your body in a defensive posture. You may notice your breath becoming shallow, held high in the chest, the diaphragm retreating as if to protect fragile internal organs from further injury.

This biological response is compounded by the cultural scripts you carry - the messages that your fury is ugly, that your anger is “too much,” that your body’s wisdom is unreliable. But the jaw remembers. It remembers the long nights when you clenched your teeth so hard you could taste blood, the silent scream held behind closed lips. It is not weakness to feel rage at being gaslit by the very hands meant to heal. It is the body’s alarm system blaring when the caretakers become the source of threat.

In this storm of physiology and emotion, your body is trying to communicate the depth of your hurt and the urgency of your need. The sympathetic nervous system’s overdrive is a survival mechanism, not a character flaw. It is a biological call to honor your experience, to listen to the muscles that tighten, the breath that catches, the skin that prickles with indignation. This is your body saying: I am here. I will not be ignored.

## Herbal Allies for Medical Trauma

When the healthcare system’s machinery grinds against your body and spirit, the rage that rises is not just mental - it plants itself deep in your flesh, tightens your muscles, and quickens your pulse. This is not some vague emotional storm; it is a biological event, a cascade of signals your body sends to make you survive. In those moments, the heart pounds in the chest like a trapped bird fluttering against ribs, the jaw clenches so tightly the masseter muscle aches by nightfall, and the neck braces - levator scapulae and trapezius straining under invisible weight. This is the terrain where specific herbs, revered in tradition and confirmed by centuries of somatic wisdom, come to stand with you. Not as magic cures, but as allies in your body’s conversation with rage born of medical trauma.

Take motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca), for example. It is an herb intricately tied to the heart - not just metaphorically but literally. When your pulse races after a humiliating dismissal by a doctor, when the sternum feels tight, and the diaphragm fights for space beneath it, motherwort can be a balm. Its bitter, slightly minty taste pulls the breath downward, encouraging the diaphragm’s slow, rhythmic expansion. Preparing motherwort as a tea is simple but sacred: steep a teaspoon of dried leaves and flowers in a cup of hot water for ten minutes, covering the vessel to keep the volatile oils intact. Sip it slowly, feeling the warmth slide down your throat and settle as a gentle coolness behind the breastbone. The ritual becomes an anchor - something steady and grounding in the chaos of medical rage.

Then there is wood betony (Stachys officinalis), a quiet, unassuming plant with deep roots in European herbal tradition. When your head feels like it’s filled with a tightening band of iron, your neck muscles locked in a vice, wood betony’s slightly grassy, mildly astringent infusion can ease the tension. It softens the fascia around the skull base and calms the levator scapulae, allowing the shoulders to drop a few millimeters - an almost imperceptible but crucial release. To prepare, steep a tablespoon of the dried herb in hot water for 8 to 12 minutes. The ritual of brewing wood betony is a slow, deliberate act, a chance to pause and feel the weight of tension itself before offering it a touch of relief.

Milky oats (Avena sativa) offer a different kind of support. When the nervous system is frayed, when the constant hypervigilance of navigating a hostile medical environment leaves you feeling raw and exposed, milky oats act as a restorative tonic. They are the herbal equivalent of a heavy, warm blanket over a shivering body. The milky tops of the oat plant, harvested just before they mature, are rich in minerals and nerve-soothing compounds. A strong infusion - steeping a handful of the dried tops in a quart of hot water overnight - yields a sweet, earthy brew that nourishes the exhausted adrenal glands. Drinking this infusion throughout the day helps to rebuild the resilience of the psoas and the deep core muscles that have been clenched in perpetual defense.

These herbs are not meant to silence your anger. They are meant to support the body that holds it. You can find [Host Defense Lion's Mane Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07TJ63MH3?tag=spankyspinola-20) or [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20) to begin this practice. When you take them, you are not trying to fix a pathology; you are honoring your biology. You are giving your masseter permission to unclench, your diaphragm space to expand, and your heart the steady rhythm it needs to keep fighting for the care you deserve.

## Somatic Practice: Grounding in the Exam Room

You are sitting on the exam table, the paper crinkling loudly beneath you with every breath. The air is cold, the fluorescent lights are buzzing, and the doctor is late. Your body is already bracing - the psoas tightening, the masseter clenching, the levator scapulae pulling your shoulders toward your ears. This is the moment to reclaim your body, right here in the sterile center of the clinical space. You do not need to close your eyes or make a show of it. This practice is quiet, internal, and entirely yours.

Begin by bringing your attention to your sit bones. Feel the hard surface of the exam table pressing against them. Notice the exact point of contact between your flesh and the vinyl beneath the paper. Let your weight drop into that contact. You are not floating in this room; you are anchored. Feel the gravity pulling you downward, a steady, undeniable force that tethers you to the earth, even in this artificial environment.

Next, shift your focus to your breath, but do not try to force it into a deep, calm rhythm if your body is resisting. Instead, simply notice where the breath is living right now. Is it trapped high in your chest? Is your diaphragm tight? Acknowledge it without judgment. Then, gently invite the breath to travel just an inch lower. Imagine the air moving down past your collarbones, past your sternum, and into the soft belly. With each inhale, let the belly expand slightly, pushing against the waistband of your clothes. With each exhale, let the breath fall out of you, a slow, deliberate release.

Now, bring your awareness to your jaw. The masseter muscle is likely holding the tension of everything you want to say but feel you cannot. You don’t need to force it open. Instead, gently part your lips just a fraction of a millimeter. Let the tongue drop away from the roof of your mouth. Feel the subtle softening in the hinges of your jaw. As you do this, notice if the tension in your neck - the levator scapulae and the trapezius - begins to shift. You are not trying to relax; you are simply giving your muscles permission to stop bracing for impact.

Finally, feel your feet on the floor or the step of the exam table. Press your toes down, then your heels. Feel the solid structure beneath you. You are here. Your body is yours. The rage you feel is valid, but in this moment, you are choosing to inhabit your flesh fully, to stand your ground from the inside out. When the door opens and the doctor walks in, you will meet them not as a collection of symptoms, but as a whole, grounded, breathing person. You have reclaimed your space, and your body is right here behind you.

## FAQ

You’re sitting in the sterile fluorescent hum of a waiting room. The clock ticks too loud against the backdrop of your own pounding heart. You wonder: “Is this rage normal? Am I broken for feeling so furious at the very people meant to heal me?” The short answer is no. Your anger in this space is neither an accident nor a moral failing. It’s a visceral, body-first signal that something in the system has violated your boundaries, left you gasping for oxygen, jaw clenched so tight the masseter muscle aches hours later, and sent your diaphragm into a tight knot beneath your ribs. This FAQ section honors that rage and helps you listen beneath it, to the message your body is shouting.

**Q: Is it common to feel rage toward doctors or healthcare providers?**

**A:** Absolutely. Medical trauma isn’t just physical injury or surgical wounds; it’s a deep, embodied experience where your body’s safety was compromised. The trapezius and levator scapulae in your neck might clench whenever you remember dismissive words or cold hands. Thich Nhat Hanh teaches us that the body remembers, and here it’s not pathology but biology doing its precise work. Your fury is a response to being unseen, unheard, or worse, harmed. It’s a protective roar from your nervous system that says, “You matter.”

**Q: How can I advocate for myself without feeling like I have to abandon my body’s signals?**

**A:** This is where many get stuck  -  between the desire for medical care and the fear of being silenced or retraumatized. Advocacy isn’t about pushing harder to be heard over the clinical noise; it’s about tuning into the bodily wisdom beneath your anger, the subtle tightening in your diaphragm or the heat pooling behind your eyes. Robert Augustus Masters reminds us that when you ground your voice in this somatic awareness, your words carry a different weight. You’re not just arguing; you’re asking your body to be a partner in the conversation. Practice naming the sensations first: “I feel my throat closing and my chest tightening when I’m interrupted. Can we slow down?” This simple truth anchors your presence without abandonment.

**Q: What if my anger makes me feel shame or guilt?**

**A:** The cultural scripts around women’s anger, especially in healthcare settings, are brutal. You were likely taught that fury is ugly, dangerous, or unlovable. Pema Chödrön’s work on leaning into discomfort can be a balm here. Your shame is not a sign you need to shrink; it’s an invitation to witness the tightness in your jaw - the masseter again - and the way your breath wants to shorten and hide. Naming these sensations without judgment allows you to hold the rage alongside compassion for yourself. This is not a failure of character but an invitation to radical honesty with your body.

**Q: Are there herbs or practices that can help soothe the body while holding this anger?**

**A:** Yes. This is not about silencing your fury but supporting your nervous system as it processes the experience. Herbs like chamomile or lavender can gently ease the tension in the diaphragm and levator scapulae, while adaptogens such as ashwagandha support resilience in the psoas and core muscles that store stress. Gabor Maté’s insights on trauma remind us that these plants are allies, not cures, helping the body to find its balance amidst chaos. Try a ritual of deep, slow breathing with a cup of [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20) before or after medical appointments, allowing the body’s edges to soften without losing the message in your rage.

**Q: How do I distinguish between justified anger and when I might be reacting from past trauma?**

**A:** This is a subtle and necessary inquiry. Resmaa Menakem’s somatic approach teaches that trauma is stored below the neck - in your nervous system’s memory of threat - and often replays in present moments as amplified rage. When you feel the familiar clench of your jaw and the flare of your nostrils, pause and ask: “Is this anger about what’s happening now, or is it an echo from before?” This doesn’t invalidate your current experience; it enriches it, adding layers of understanding. You can hold both: the present violation and the ancestral or personal wounds carried in your fascia. Your body knows this. Listening deeply is the first step toward reclaiming your power in the healthcare system.

In all of this, remember: your anger is not a problem to fix but a messenger to honor. The muscles of your body - the masseter, trapezius, diaphragm - are speaking. Your task is to listen, to hold, and to act from a place where your body is neither abandoned nor silenced. This is how you survive healthcare rage and transform it into fierce, embodied advocacy.
